{"query":"Debian Docker images xz backdoor Binarly August 2025 CVE-2024-3094 findings removal status","model":"mako","response_time_ms":1767,"verdict":null,"extra_info":null,"results":[{"url":"https://www.binarly.io/blog/persistent-risk-xz-utils-backdoor-still-lurking-docker","title":"Persistent Risk: XZ Utils Backdoor Still Lurking in Docker Images | Binarly","content":"Book a live tour to watch Binarly validate SBOM/CBOM, surface exploitable risks with reachability and exploitation maturity scoring, and chart a measurable path to post‑quantum readiness."},{"url":"https://www.penligent.ai/hackinglabs/cve-2024-3094-xz-utils-backdoor-and-the-liblzma-trap-door/","title":"CVE-2024-3094, XZ Utils Backdoor and the liblzma Trap Door","content":"It is a supply-chain compromise where malicious code was discovered in upstream XZ release tarballs beginning with 5.6.0, delivered through a build-time injection path that modifies liblzma during compilation.
The 2025 reports about vulnerable XZ remnants in Docker Hub images are a useful reminder: even when distros revert quickly, old layers and old tags can persist and be re-used.
systemctl status ssh 2>/dev/null || systemctl status sshd 2>/dev/null || true systemctl cat ssh 2>/dev/null || systemctl cat sshd 2>/dev/null || true
Treat this as a supply-chain compromise, not a conventional vulnerability NVD explicitly describes malicious code in upstream tarballs and a build-time injection that produces modified liblzma functions.
A short, useful comparison: CVE-2024-3094 vs Log4Shell
Log4Shell (CVE-2021-44228) was an application-layer vulnerability with broad internet reachability in many environments.
CVE-2026-58644 is a critical remote code execution vulnerability in on-premises Microsoft SharePoint Server."},{"url":"https://unit42.paloaltonetworks.com/threat-brief-xz-utils-cve-2024-3094/","title":"Threat Brief: XZ Utils Vulnerability (CVE-2024-3094)","content":"On March 28, 2024, Red Hat Linux announced CVE-2024-3094 with a critical CVSS score of 10.
The Next-Generation Firewall with cloud-delivered security services including Advanced WildFire detects the compromised versions described in this report as malicious, as well as features known to be associated with the backdoors.
Versions 5.6.0 and 5.6.1 of the libraries contain malicious code that modifies functions during the liblzma build process.
However, if you did not update your Kali installation before March 26, you are not affected by this backdoor vulnerability.
They do not believe Homebrew’s builds were compromised but are taking this action as a precaution.
Amazon has stated that Amazon Linux customers are not affected by this issue, and no action is required.
The Unit 42 Managed Threat Hunting team continues to track any attempted malicious activities across relevant Linux distributions used by our customers, using Cortex XDR and the XQL queries below.
Cortex XDR customers can also use these XQL queries to search for affected versions of XZ Utils.
Based on the information presently known, Palo Alto Networks products and cloud services do not contain affected XZ software packages and are not impacted by these issues.
For example, to upgrade a vulnerable HomeBrew installation on a list of Macs to the latest version (with the downgraded, safe, version of XZ) you can execute the following commands via XDR on your endpoints.
Brew install xz installs the outdated version 5.4.6 instead of 5.6.1 – Homebrew, Discussion #5243 on GitHub
PT to add info on Advanced WildFire protections."}]}

You can’t perform that action at this time.","score":0.8506799,"raw_content":null},{"url":"https://www.darkreading.com/vulnerabilities-threats/xz-utils-backdoor-live-old-docker-images","title":"Whispers of XZ Utils Backdoor Live on in Old Docker Images","content":"# Whispers of XZ Utils Backdoor Live on in Old Docker Images. Developers maintaining the images made the \"intentional choice\" to leave the artifacts available as \"a historical curiosity,\" given the improbability they'd be exploited. Binarly on Aug. 12 published research concerning the XY Utils backdoor, a notorious incident in which a developer known as \"Jia Tan\" spent two years building trust and credibility developing for open source lossless data compressor XZ Utils, only to then build a malicious Linux backdoor into the project. According to Binarly researchers, \"several Docker images built around the time of the compromise contain the backdoor,\" and some are still available via the container image library Docker Hub. Although its search for backdoors was not comprehensive, researchers identified 35 Debian images on Docker Hub that contained the backdoor. \"The impact on Docker images from Fedora, OpenSUSE, and other distributions that were impacted by the XZ Utils backdoor remains unknown at this
